text:
We present redMaPPer, a new generation of red-sequence cluster finder optimized for the analysis of large photometric surveys. The technique as it applies to SDSS and DES consists of the following innovations: (1) an iterative self-training procedure which enables the calibration of the red-sequence model using only a small fraction of the spectroscopic data; (2) handling of survey masks and depth variations in a fully integrated manner; (stronger) production of cluster-specific random catalogues for large-scale structure studies; and (4) full redshift probability distributions, $P(z)$, for all clusters, as well as a probabilistic centering procedure which identifies the top candidate central galaxies along with their centering weights.

The redMaPPer algorithm is numerically efficient and highly parallel, and is able to process a DES-sized catalog in roughly 500 CPU hours. We validate the high precision of the photometric redshifts and the excellent correlation of the estimated richness with independent mass estimates, resulting in highly pure and complete galaxy cluster catalogs.

We apply redMaPPer to ~10,000 deg^2 of SDSS DR8 data, to obtain a catalog of ∼25,000 galaxy clusters over the redshift range 0.08 < z < 0.55. The redshift distributions are nearly Gaussian with a scatter of σz ∼ 0.006 at z∼0.1, increasing to σz ∼ 0.02 at z∼0.5 as the photometry approaches the depth limit of the survey. The median |Δz|/(1+z) is 0.006, and is low for large z; the contamination due to projections is also low, at ≤5%. In Appendix A and Rozo & Rykoff (2014) we provide systematic comparisons with X-ray and SZ-detected clusters, as available for our sample.
